sections.content: This time 265 cells changed in a large bbox [0,28]-[63,60]. This is much more significant than just the timer bar. Let me check what actually changed in the gameplay objects.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking the blue block
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

def get_objects(seg):
    objs = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        objs.append({
            'color': n['color'],
            'pixels': n['pixels'],
            'hash': n['hash'],
            'bbox': (min(rows), min(cols), max(rows), max(cols))
        })
    return objs

before_objs = get_objects(before)
after_objs = get_objects(after)

print("Before:")
for o in before_objs:
    print(f"  color={o['color']} pixels={o['pixels']} bbox={o['bbox']}")
print("\nAfter:")
for o in after_objs:
    print(f"  color={o['color']} pixels={o['pixels']} bbox={o['bbox']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Significant changes happened:
1. Timer: P went from 61→60 pixels (as expected)
2. Gray (G) region: pixels 2580→2600, bbox changed from (1,0,63,51) to (1,0,63,55) - the gray expanded right by 4 columns (from col 51 to col 55)
3. Upper white (W): pixels 848→956, bbox changed from (1,32,27,63) to (1,28,27,63) - the white expanded left by 4 columns (from col 32 to col 28)
4. Lower white (W): pixels 368→240, bbox changed from (32,52,63,63) to (32,56,63,63) - the white shrank left by 4 columns (from col 52 to col 56)
5. Charcoal cross (c): bbox changed from (44,46,49,49) to (44,50,49,53) - moved right by 4 columns
6. Yellow strip (Y): bbox changed from (44,50,49,51) to (44,54,49,55) - moved right by 4 columns
So clicking the blue block shifted the gray/white boundary and moved the charcoal cross + yellow strip to the right by 4 columns! The gray area expanded rightward, pushing the white area and the charcoal cross rightward.
The charcoal cross and yellow strip moved from columns 46-49 and 50-51 to 50-53 and 54-55, confirming a 4-column rightward shift. This suggests the blue blocks act as directional triggers that move the charcoal cross along the board, with the goal likely being to navigate it to a specific target position.
The upper blue block I clicked produced this rightward movement. I should test whether the lower blue block creates the opposite effect, shifting the cross back to the left.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the upper blue block shifted the charcoal cross + yellow strip RIGHT by 4 columns (from cols 46-49 to 50-53). The gray/white boundary also shifted right. Blue blocks are directional controls!
Goal model: Move the charcoal cross to a target position. The upper blue block moves it right.
Plan: Test if the lower blue block moves it left, then figure out the target destination.
